moveAnnotations.annotation: We're starting with the unusual Sodium Attack, an offbeat opening that immediately places our knight on the rim—normally a dubious square. This move is played for shock value and to avoid main-line theory, hoping to seize the initiative through unconventional play. The knight on a3 eyes the b5 and c4 squares, potentially supporting a later b4 pawn push, but we must be careful not to waste time with this piece placement. This is a weapon for practical players who want quick wins against unprepared opponents rather than fighting for equality.

Black immediately signals their intention to control the center and long diagonal with a fianchetto setup. By moving the g-pawn and preparing to develop the bishop to g7, Black is essentially ignoring our unusual opening choice and implementing their own solid plan. This is a sensible response that doesn't fall into the trap of trying to refute the Sodium Attack—instead, Black just develops harmoniously and keeps their position flexible.

We're committed to an aggressive, flank-based expansion, securing space on the kingside and preventing Black's fianchettoed bishop from controlling key central squares. By advancing g4, we're claiming territory and potentially preparing h4-h5 to disrupt Black's kingside setup, though we've seriously weakened our kingside defenses and our king is becoming vulnerable. This move shows the double-edged nature of the Sodium Attack: we're playing ambitiously and creating immediate threats, but we're also walking a tightrope where accurate play from Black can quickly exploit our loose king position.
